results.possibleIdentity:
Phyllis Gershuny (now known as Phyllis Segura) is a multi-disciplinary American artist, painter, and editor who was a central figure in the early video art movement of the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is most famously recognized as a co-founder and editor of 'Radical Software' (1970), a seminal journal for video art and cybernetic culture, which she established alongside Beryl Korot and Ira Schneider under the Raindance Corporation. The publication was a primary forum for the emerging video community and promoted the idea of decentralizing information through technology.

Gershuny's career began with formal art training at Boston University and the Art Students League, followed by private studies with notable artists such as Nicholas Carone and Jordan Belson. In the late 1960s, she lived in Europe where she worked with the Living Theater and was introduced to video by British journalist John Hopkins. Upon returning to New York, she became a strategist for the video art scene, participating in the first museum exhibition of video art, 'Vision and Television' (1970) at the Rose Art Museum.

In the 1970s, she engaged in pioneering experiments in holography and video in San Francisco at the School of Holography. Her later artistic practice includes painting, textile design—having taught at the Fashion Institute of Technology (F.I.T.)—and founding 'Broadshirt,' a poetry magazine printed on T-shirts. She also studied Tibetan Thangka painting and Zen archery. Her role as a pioneer in art and technology was recently celebrated in the survey exhibition 'Radical Software: Women, Art & Computing 1960–1991' (2024–2025).

results.possibleIdentity: Paul J. Smith (1931–2020) was a prominent New York-based arts administrator, curator, and artist who served as the director of the Museum of Contemporary Crafts (now the Museum of Arts and Design) from 1963 to 1987. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he curated several groundbreaking exhibitions that promoted interdisciplinary collaborations between artists, designers, and engineers, aligning with the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Notable exhibitions included "Body Covering" (1968), which explored the intersection of clothing, technology, and performance, and "Plastic as Plastic" (1969), which highlighted the creative use of industrial materials. He also curated "Contemplation Environments" (1970), which featured the work of E.A.T. associated artist Aleksandra Kasuba. Smith was a key figure in the 20th-century studio craft movement and frequently collaborated with various organizations to integrate emerging technologies into the arts.

results.possibleIdentity: Davidson Gigliotti (born 1939) is an American video artist, art historian, and a pioneer of the early video art movement. Active since the late 1960s, he was a founding member of the influential video collective Videofreex (1969–1978) and the associated group Media Bus. Gigliotti is closely associated with the early history of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), often documenting its impact on the development of video as an art form and participating in the interdisciplinary community it fostered. He collaborated with sculptor Pedro Lujan to build an inflatable projection environment for early media art events, such as the 1966 USCO show. His artistic practice focuses on multi-channel video installations and single-channel tapes that explore urban and rural environments, most notably in his 1972 work 'Quaking Aspens' and the 1979 piece 'After Montgolfier.' From 1975 to 1987, he served as the Director of Video at the Experimental Intermedia Foundation in New York. His work has been exhibited at major institutions including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art (including the 1981 Whitney Biennial), and is held in the collection of the ZKM Center for Art and Media Karlsruhe.

results.possibleIdentity: F. Clifford Rose (Frank Clifford Rose, 1926–2012) was a prominent British neurologist and author who specialized in the intersection of neuroscience and the arts. He was the Founding Director of the Academic Unit of Neuroscience at Charing Cross and Westminster Medical School (now part of Imperial College London). Rose authored and edited several seminal works that explored the relationship between science, technology, and art, including "The Neurobiology of Painting" (2006), "Neurology of the Arts: Painting, Music, Literature" (2004), and "Neurology of Music" (2010). His research frequently focused on how neurological processes and brain damage influence artistic creation and perception, a field often referred to as neuroaesthetics. He was active as a medical researcher and author from the late 1960s through the early 21st century. His work is often cited in the context of the historical emergence of "neuro-art" in the 1960s and 1970s, which aligned with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) that promoted collaborations between artists and scientists.

Clifford Rose (John Clifford Rose, 1929–2021) was a distinguished British stage, television, and film actor who was an Honorary Associate Artist of the Royal Shakespeare Company (RSC). He was a founding member of the RSC in 1960 and remained active with the company throughout the late 1960s and 1970s. While primarily known for his acting, particularly his portrayal of Ludwig Kessler in the BBC series "Secret Army" (1977–1979) and its sequel "Kessler" (1981), his status as an Associate Artist and his involvement in experimental theater during the 1960s (such as Peter Brook's "Marat/Sade" in 1964 and 1967) place him within the active artistic community of that era. His work in the late 1960s included television roles in series like "Callan" (1967–1972) and various stage productions at Stratford-upon-Avon.

results.possibleIdentity: Jon Hassell (1937–2021) was an American trumpeter, composer, and influential musical innovator best known for developing the "Fourth World" genre—a fusion of traditional ethnic music with modern electronic techniques. He was highly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, a period in which he collaborated with major figures in the avant-garde and minimalist movements. Hassell studied under Karlheinz Stockhausen in Cologne (1965–1967) and later worked with Terry Riley, performing on the seminal 1968 recording of "In C." He was also a member of La Monte Young's Theatre of Eternal Music and studied Indian classical music with vocalist Pandit Pran Nath. Hassell had a direct connection to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; he contributed a piece to an E.A.T. exhibition in New York, which featured a visual demonstration of tape delay built with the assistance of Robert Moog. His career continued through the decades, most famously collaborating with Brian Eno on the 1980 album "Fourth World, Vol. 1: Possible Musics," which codified his signature sound of processed trumpet and global rhythmic textures.

results.possibleIdentity: James Kent Rundell, Jr. (often referred to as Kent Rundell) is an American artist who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He graduated from Michigan State University (MSU) in 1972 with a bachelor's degree in Art Practice. During his time at MSU, he was part of a cohort of students and faculty engaged in experimental and technical art practices. His active years coincide with the peak activity of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, a non-profit dedicated to fostering collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists. While he is documented as an 'Art Practice' graduate, he is also associated with the artistic community in Michigan, where a Kent Rundell (likely him or his father) was later recognized as an 'Elder' and community leader, serving as the President of the Flushing Lions Club in 1980–1981 and becoming a prominent figure in the National Horseshoe Pitchers Association (NHPA) in Michigan.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephen Kaltenbach (born 1940) is an American conceptual artist who was a prominent figure in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Battle Creek, Michigan, he earned his BA and MA at the University of California, Davis, where he studied under Robert Arneson and William T. Wiley. Moving to New York in 1967, Kaltenbach became known for his innovative conceptual strategies that emphasized anonymity, time, and architectural space. His notable early works include 'Time Capsules' (sealed metal containers with instructions to be opened in the future), anonymous 'micro-manifesto' advertisements placed in Artforum magazine (e.g., 'Become a Legend'), and bronze 'Sidewalk Plaques' embedded in public walkways. He participated in several seminal exhibitions of the era, including 'Nine' at the Leo Castelli Warehouse (1968), Harald Szeemann’s 'When Attitudes Become Form' (1969), and the 'Information' show at the Museum of Modern Art (1970). In 1970, at the height of his New York career, he abruptly moved to Sacramento, California—a move he later characterized as a conceptual project titled the 'Elephant Project' intended to subvert his own reputation. In Sacramento, he adopted the persona of a 'Regional Artist,' focusing on figurative sculpture and long-term projects such as 'Portrait of My Father' (1972–1979). He taught at California State University, Sacramento, from 1970 until 2005. His work is included in major collections such as the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art.

results.possibleIdentity: Donald "Don" Little is a performing artist and educator who was active in the early 1970s and is known for his work as a tubist and his collaborations with major avant-garde artists associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was a member of the improvisatory chamber ensemble BL Lacerta, founded in 1976 at North Texas State University (now University of North Texas). Through BL Lacerta, Little collaborated with core E.A.T. figures such as John Cage and Deborah Hay—two of the original ten artists who participated in the seminal 1966 E.A.T. project '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering.' Little also worked with other experimental pioneers like Pauline Oliveros and Stuart Dempster. Professionally, he has served as the Regents Professor of Tuba at the University of North Texas since 1976 and was previously a faculty member at the University of Northern Iowa. He was the principal tubist for the Fort Worth Symphony Orchestra and the Dallas Opera Orchestra for 45 years.

Don Little was an engineer and designer active in the mid-1960s who specialized in experimental technical systems, fitting the profile of the technical collaborators sought by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1966, he served as the designer of the unique control system for the Westinghouse Sky Bus, an automated transit experiment in Pittsburgh. During the same period, another Don Little headed the instrumentation and photography section at the Suffield Experimental Station, a major Canadian research center involved in large-scale experimental trials. E.A.T. was founded on the principle of pairing such engineers and technical specialists with artists to explore new media and technologies.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born 1931) is a Hungarian-born American conceptual artist often cited as A. Denes. Active since the late 1960s, she is a pioneer of Environmental and Land art. Her work often integrates science, philosophy, and mathematics, a practice she terms 'Visual Philosophy.' In the late 1960s, she became a member of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) collective, and her involvement is documented in the organization's archives from 1969 to 1970. One of her earliest significant works was 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (1968), recognized as a foundational piece of ecological art. She was also a featured artist in the 1970 'Software' exhibition at the Jewish Museum in New York, which explored the intersection of information technology and art. Her multidisciplinary approach includes large-scale environmental projects, holograms, computer-generated prints, and philosophical drawings.
